pairs.question: Where and when did the 1964 Democratic National Convention take place?
pairs.answer: The 1964 Democratic National Convention was held at Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City, New Jersey, from August 24 to 27, 1964.

pairs.question: Who was nominated for president and vice president at the 1964 Democratic National Convention?
pairs.answer: President Lyndon B. Johnson was nominated for a full term, and Senator Hubert H. Humphrey of Minnesota was nominated for vice president.

pairs.question: How did President Johnson respond to civil rights activists at the 1964 Democratic National Convention?
pairs.answer: Johnson ordered FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover to monitor civil rights activists, resulting in wiretapping, bugging, and infiltration of their groups.

pairs.question: What notable speeches or events occurred on the first day of the 1964 Democratic National Convention?
pairs.answer: Senator John O. Pastore delivered a keynote speech, passionately discussing the party's success and remembrance of President John F. Kennedy. The speech was later featured on the cover of The New York Times and Life magazine.

pairs.question: What happened when Robert F. Kennedy appeared on the convention floor on the last day?
pairs.answer: Delegates gave him 22 minutes of uninterrupted applause, causing him to nearly break into tears. He quoted from Romeo and Juliet: 'When he shall die, take him and cut him out into the stars, and he shall make the face of heaven so fine that all the world will be in love with night and pay no worship to the garish sun.'

pairs.question: What role did Eleanor Roosevelt play in the 1964 Democratic National Convention?
pairs.answer: Ambassador Adlai E. Stevenson II introduced a memorial film for Eleanor Roosevelt, who had died on November 7, 1962. She received a short but polite ovation before the film.

pairs.question: How did retirees influence the 1964 Democratic National Convention, and what was the outcome?
pairs.answer: On August 26, 1964, 14,000 retirees from the National Council of Senior Citizens arrived to encourage President Johnson to extend public health insurance. This advocacy contributed to the passage of Medicare in July 1965.
